ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for braised pork rice as a composite dish; FOODON_03315595 (braised pork dish) used as closest defensible anchor. Rice component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (pork dish) used as closest defensible category for braised pork rice composite. No exact FoodEx2 entry for lu rou fan as a named dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for braised pork rice as a composite dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared dishes at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre serving of braised pork rice is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of white rice topped with braised pork belly slices, braising sauce, and optionally a braised egg and preserved vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Pork Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Braised Pork Rice (G00010396), confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB entry; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork belly and white rice composite proportions and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised pork rice as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice (jasmine/long-grain), which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The pork belly and braising sauce contribute negligible additional carbohydrate-driven GI elevation. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 88.2) / 100 ≈ 63.5, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 25.19 × 3.5 = 88.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and oyster sauce in braising liquid; composite sodium of 231 mg/100 g likely underestimates true per-serving sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid braised pork rice due to its high sodium content from soy-based braising sauces; request sauce on the side or reduced-sodium preparation, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with 25.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g produces a high glycemic load per serving; pork belly saturated fat worsens insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit braised pork rice due to its high glycemic load from white rice and the insulin-resistance-worsening effect of pork belly saturated fat; substituting brown rice, reducing portion size, and requesting leaner pork cuts are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork belly is high in saturated fat contributing to LDL elevation; high sodium from braising sauce raises blood pressure; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100 g in HPB composite but pork belly inherently contains dietary cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit braised pork rice due to its saturated fat content from pork belly and high sodium from soy-based braising sauce, both of which adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure; leaner protein substitutions and reduced-sauce preparations are advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly is high in saturated fat and arachidonic acid (omega-6), both pro-inflammatory; high sodium from braising sauce worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit braised pork rice due to the pro-inflammatory saturated fat and arachidonic acid content of pork belly and the sodium burden from soy-based braising sauce; increasing vegetable intake alongside the meal may provide partial anti-inflammatory offset.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Pork Rice (卤肉饭 / 滷肉飯, romanized: Lu Rou Fan or Lor Bak Png in Hokkien) is a popular Singaporean and Taiwanese hawker dish consisting of steamed white rice topped with slow-braised pork belly cooked in a rich, savory-sweet sauce made from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, five-spice powder, garlic, shallots, and sugar. The pork belly is braised until tender, yielding a melt-in-the-mouth texture with a deeply umami, slightly sweet, and aromatic flavor profile. The dish is commonly served with a braised hard-boiled egg, preserved vegetables (chai poh), and blanched greens. In Singapore, it is a staple hawker-centre and coffee-shop dish available at lunch and dinner. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with 231 mg sodium and 3.7 g dietary fibre. The dish carries a high glycemic index (~72) due to its white rice base and a high glycemic load per standard 350 g serving (~63.5). Clinically, it is a concern for individuals with hypertension (high sodium from braising sauces), Type 2 Diabetes (high-GI carbohydrate load and saturated fat worsening insulin resistance), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from pork belly and sodium burden). The dish is not halal due to pork content and contains soy and gluten allergens from soy sauce and oyster sauce.
common_user_queries: Is lor bak png suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat braised pork rice if I have high blood pressure?
What is lu rou fan made of?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Braised Pork Rice (G00010396) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork belly and white rice proportional composition. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white rice analogue (GI 72), which is the dominant carbohydrate driver. All mandatory multilingual names are present. Ontology anchors are defensible approximations with gap notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central pork belly (NDB 10006) and cooked white rice (NDB 20444) proportional composition, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish. This value should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis when available. 2. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g in the HPB composite record. This is the HPB-reported value and is preserved as-is; however, pork belly inherently contains dietary cholesterol, and this figure likely reflects a composite averaging artifact. cholesterol_mg is set to 0.0 per the HPB source; this is a confirmed source-reported value, not a naturally zero nutrient. 3. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because the data is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this composite dish (G00010396) as of April 2026, and USDA FoodData Central does not contain an exact match for this Singaporean composite preparation: monounsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this dish; polyunsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this dish; trans_fat_g — trans fat not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_c_mg — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_b6_mg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_b12_mcg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this composite dish; folate_mcg_dfe — folate not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_d_mcg — vitamin D not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_e_mg — vitamin E not reported by HPB for this composite dish; phosphorus_mg — phosphorus not reported by HPB for this composite dish; zinc_mg — zinc not reported by HPB for this composite dish; selenium_mcg — selenium not reported by HPB for this composite dish; water_g — moisture content not reported by HPB for this composite dish. Source consulted and confirmed unavailable: HPB Singapore Foodo (G00010396, accessed April 2026) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (no exact match for this Singaporean composite preparation). 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (jasmine/long-grain) from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); no direct GI measurement for braised pork rice as a composite dish is available in any approved GI source. 5. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315595 and FoodEx2 code A0FJP are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for braised pork rice as a named composite dish. 6. The dish is not halal due to pork content. 7. Sodium of 231 mg/100 g from HPB may underestimate true per-serving sodium given the soy sauce and oyster sauce braising liquid; actual per-serving sodium for a 350 g plate may exceed 800 mg.
